cards.definition: The recommended depth of the layer when whole cherry is spread out on a drying yard.
cards.analogy: Thin enough for air to move through the pile, thick enough not to waste yard space — a balance, not a guess.
cards.why_it_matters: Too thick and the inside never dries evenly; too thin and yard capacity is wasted for no quality gain.
cards.cloze: Cherry coffee should be spread uniformly in drying yards at a thickness of ___ cm.

cards.definition: The moisture percentage whole cherry coffee should be dried down to before storage — a different target than parchment's, since cherry retains the whole fruit.
cards.analogy: A slightly different finish line than parchment drying, because there's more material (the whole fruit) holding water.
cards.why_it_matters: Confirms cherry and parchment aren't interchangeable numbers — asking which process a stated moisture figure refers to matters.
cards.cloze: Cherry coffee should be dried to a moisture level of ___%.

cards.definition: The auditory test — alongside a two-day stable-weight check — used to confirm whole cherry has finished drying.
cards.analogy: The same instinct as shaking a box of dried beans to hear if they move freely, versus a damp, muffled thud.
cards.why_it_matters: A quick field test that, paired with the weight check, avoids relying on a moisture meter alone.
cards.cloze: Dried cherry coffee is confirmed ready when a handful produces a ___ sound when shaken and a sample records the same weight on two consecutive days.
